title: The World Spent $2.88 Trillion on Armies Last Year — That's $350 per Person!
summary: Have you ever wondered how much the world spends on soldiers, tanks, and warships? Last year, the total was a jaw-dropping $2.88 trillion! That's enough to give every single person on the planet $350. A group called SIPRI keeps track of this. They say the five biggest spenders are the United States, China, Russia, Germany, and India. The U.S. alone spent $954 billion — more than the next six countries combined. In fact, since 1949, the U.S. has spent over $53 trillion on its military, which is more than half of all the money the whole world has spent on armies in that time. Military spending usually goes up during wars and down during peaceful times. After World War II, it shot up because of the Korean War. Then it stayed high during the Cold War, when the U.S. and Soviet Union were rivals. It fell after the Cold War ended, but rose again after the 9/11 attacks and the wars in Afghanistan and Iraq. In the last 10 years, spending has climbed again, especially in Europe after Russia took over Crimea in 2014. Some countries spend a lot per person. Qatar spends the most — $5,428 per person! Israel is second at $5,108. Ukraine's spending jumped by 3,387% because of its war with Russia. All this money for weapons means less for things like schools and hospitals. So next time you hear about a new fighter jet, remember: that's a choice countries make with their money.
why_it_matters: Every dollar spent on a tank or fighter jet is a dollar that can’t be spent on your school, local library, or playground, so these choices shape the world you grow up in.

keywords.explanation: A research group in Sweden that counts how much countries spend on their militaries.
A huge number that means one thousand billion.
A long period of dangerous rivalry between the United States and the Soviet Union after World War II.
A piece of land on the Black Sea that Russia took from Ukraine in 2014.
Money a country uses to pay for soldiers, weapons, and warships.
A small but very rich country in the Middle East that spends more per person on defense than any other nation.

questions.question: How much did the world spend on militaries last year, in total?
Which country spent the most on its military last year?
Since 1949, how much has the U.S. alone spent on its military?
Which country spends the most per person on its military?
By how much did Ukraine’s military spending jump because of its war with Russia?
What organization tracks worldwide military spending?

background_read: During the Cold War (the long standoff between the U.S. and the Soviet Union that lasted from about 1947 to 1991), both sides built enormous armies and stockpiles of nuclear weapons.
After Russia took over Crimea (a peninsula on the Black Sea) from Ukraine in 2014, many European countries began spending more on defense out of worry over similar actions.
Although the global total hit $2.88 trillion last year, that money could have been used to build more schools, hospitals, or parks.
Article_Structure: WHO: The world’s biggest defense spenders — the United States, China, Russia, Germany, and India — along with watchdog group SIPRI.
WHAT: Last year the world spent a record‑like $2.88 trillion on armies, an amount equal to about $350 for every person on Earth.
WHERE: Everywhere, from Qatar (the highest per‑person spender) to Ukraine (where spending soared) to Israel and the United States.
WHY: Wars, historic rivalries, and fears of future conflicts drive countries to pour money into weapons — a choice that means fewer resources for things like education and healthcare.

perspectives.description: Some leaders argue that strong militaries keep countries safe and stop wars before they start, so high spending is a necessary protection.
Many people say the trillions spent on weapons is wasteful and could instead help end poverty, improve health, and fight climate change.
Groups like SIPRI simply count the numbers so that citizens and governments can see how money priorities change over time and ask better questions.
